items: A **end-to-end data science project** that integrates all course skills
You choose a real-world problem, collect data, analyse it, build a model, and present findings
Designed to demonstrate **industry-relevant** competency — not just technical exercises
Completed over **2 weeks** (part-time), delivered as a 5-minute video presentation
Three structured briefs are provided — or propose your own
**Problem framing** — translate a real-world question into a data science task
**Reproducibility** — others can run your code end-to-end without hand-holding
**Communication** — present findings clearly to a non-technical audience
**Full pipeline** — data collection → cleaning → EDA → modelling → insights
Demonstrate both **technical competency** and **business acumen**

slides.title: You Should Already Be Able To...

items: Load and clean tabular data in **pandas** (Module 1–2)
Build at least one **visualisation** with clear intent (Module 1, 3)
Write and run **Python** scripts and Jupyter notebooks (Module 1.2)
Explain the **limitations** of a model or summary statistic (Module 3–4)
Apply basic **machine learning** techniques with scikit-learn (Module 5)
The capstone is **not** the place to learn pandas or SQL from scratch
Use earlier modules to **refresh** concepts before diving in
Focus your energy on **problem framing, reproducibility, and storytelling**
Poor model performance is acceptable — **methodology and communication** matter more
If you are unsure about prerequisites, revisit Modules 1–5 before Week 1

slides.title: Project Options
slides.subtitle: Three structured briefs — or bring your own

slides.title: Option A: UN SDGs Data Analysis Pipeline

items: **Goal**: Analyse progress towards specific UN Sustainable Development Goals
**Data**: UN Data Commons API — 210+ indicators across 193+ countries
**Complementary**: World Bank Open Data for socioeconomic context
Pick **2–3 focused goals** (e.g., SDG 1: No Poverty, SDG 3: Good Health)
Build predictive models to **forecast SDG progress** regionally or globally
Deliverable includes **policy recommendations** backed by data
Integrate data from **multiple sources** via APIs and direct downloads
Apply data preprocessing and **feature engineering** at scale
Create **policy-relevant visualisations** from international datasets
Build regression or classification models for **progress forecasting**

slides.title: Option B: Bahrain Vision 2030 Economic Analysis

items: **Goal**: Assess Bahrain's economic progress towards Vision 2030 targets
**Data**: data.gov.bh — general economic indicators, agricultural sector data
**Complementary**: Ministry of Finance reports, World Bank Bahrain data
Key indicators: GDP growth, private sector share, employment, trade diversification
Build **forecasting models** to project indicators towards 2030 targets
Deliverable includes an **interactive dashboard** and policy recommendations
Work with **government open data portals** and structured CSV exports
Analyse **economic trends** and time series patterns
Create visualisations that support **policy decision-making**
Apply **statsmodels** or scikit-learn for economic indicator forecasting
Conduct comparative analysis against **regional benchmarks**

slides.title: Option C: Web Scraping to Data Pipeline

items: **Goal**: Build an end-to-end pipeline — scrape, process, model, present
**Sub-option 1**: Real estate market analysis (property listings → price prediction)
**Sub-option 2**: Financial markets dashboard (Yahoo Finance → stock performance)
**Sub-option 3**: Weather & climate analysis (OpenWeatherMap → forecasting)
Deliverable includes a **working web application or dashboard** (Streamlit/Flask)
Must demonstrate a **complete, reproducible pipeline** from raw data to insight
Always **respect robots.txt** — check before scraping any site
Implement **rate limiting** (delays between requests) to avoid overloading servers
Use only **publicly available data** — do not bypass login walls
Follow the **terms of service** for all APIs and data sources
Document your data sources and access method in your README
You may propose a custom project if you have a domain interest or dataset in mind
It must satisfy **all general requirements** (collection, EDA, modelling, presentation)
Python is **strongly recommended** — R is acceptable with clear justification
Discuss your idea with your instructor **before committing** to avoid scope creep
Pre-trained models are allowed if you demonstrate understanding and cite sources

slides.title: Project Requirements
slides.subtitle: Five core areas for all options

items: Source and document your dataset(s) — record origin URL and access date
Identify and resolve **data quality issues** (missing values, duplicates, outliers)
Apply appropriate **preprocessing** techniques (normalisation, encoding, etc.)
Split data correctly — **no data leakage** between train and test sets
Store raw and processed data in clearly labelled folders
Conduct **statistical summaries** — distributions, correlations, skew
Create **at least 3 meaningful visualisations** with clear titles and labels
Extract and articulate **key insights** — what patterns surprised you?
Explore relationships between features before modelling
Document findings with markdown cells — notebooks should read like a report
Apply at least one **appropriate ML technique** (regression, classification, clustering)
Start with a **baseline model** before adding complexity
Evaluate performance using **metrics appropriate to the problem** (e.g., RMSE vs. F1)
Use **cross-validation** or a held-out test set — not just training accuracy
Document your **model selection rationale** — why this algorithm?
Present **clear findings** — what does the data tell us about the problem?
Support every conclusion with **evidence from the data or model output**
Discuss **practical implications** — what actions could a stakeholder take?
Acknowledge **limitations** — what can your model not do or predict reliably?
Suggest **future improvements** — more data, different features, better models
Use **industry-standard tools**: Python, pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib / plotly
Organise code across **numbered notebooks** (1-eda, 2-preprocessing, 3-modeling)
Follow **PEP 8 style** and include comments explaining non-obvious steps
Include a **requirements.txt** so others can reproduce your environment
Test that your notebooks run **top-to-bottom without errors** in a clean environment

slides.title: Timeline & Deliverables
slides.subtitle: Two weeks, structured day by day

items: **Days 1–2**: Explore your data source — API docs, portal, or scraping target
**Days 3–4**: Extract and load data — API calls, downloads, or scraping scripts
**Days 5–7**: Data cleaning, preprocessing, and feature engineering
By end of Week 1 you should have a **clean, analysis-ready dataset**
Commit your work to GitHub daily — no last-minute uploads
**Days 8–10**: EDA — visualisations and statistical analysis
**Days 11–12**: Model building, evaluation, and iteration
**Days 13–14**: Final presentation recording and repository clean-up
Record a **5-minute video** walking through your project (problem → results)
Final check: does everything run cleanly in a fresh environment?
**Problem Introduction (1 min)** — context, motivation, problem statement, expected impact
**Technical Approach (2 min)** — data processing, analysis techniques, model development
**Results & Insights (1.5 min)** — key findings, model performance, business recommendations
**Conclusion (0.5 min)** — achievements, future improvements, lessons learned

slides.title: Assessment & Common Pitfalls
slides.subtitle: What markers look for — and what to avoid

items: **Data quality** — are issues identified and handled appropriately?
**EDA depth** — do visualisations reveal genuine insight, not just describe data?
**Modelling rigour** — is there a baseline? Is evaluation methodology sound?
**Communication** — is the story clear to a non-technical audience?
**Reproducibility** — can a marker clone and run the repo without modifications?
**Documentation** — are decisions explained with reasoning, not just code?
**Not exploring data before modelling** — always do EDA first
**Ignoring missing values or outliers** — document what you found and what you did
**Data leakage** — never let test-set information influence training
**No baseline model** — always start simple before going complex
**Overfitting** — validate on held-out data, not just training accuracy
**Wrong metrics** — accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets; choose carefully
**Exceeding the 5-minute limit** — practice and time yourself beforehand
**Too much technical jargon** — explain for a general business audience
**Poor visual design** — clear labels, legible fonts, high contrast
**No coherent story** — connect problem → data → findings → recommendation
**Insufficient code comments** — explain the *why*, not just the *what*
**No discussion of limitations** — acknowledging weaknesses shows critical thinking
Create a **public repository** named `capstone-project` (or similar)
Folder structure: `data/`, `notebooks/` (1-eda, 2-preprocessing, 3-modeling), `src/`, `docs/`
**README.md** must include: problem statement, dataset description, methodology, key findings, setup instructions
Include **requirements.txt** listing all Python dependencies
All notebooks must be committed with **outputs visible** (run before pushing)
Record using **OBS Studio**, Loom, or any screen-recording tool
Upload to **YouTube, Vimeo, or Google Drive** — unlisted or public
Ensure the link is **accessible without login** before submitting
Submit both the **repository URL** and **video URL** via the course platform
Recommended: use **Canva or slides** to support your narrative visually
Repository is **public** and all code is committed
Notebooks run **top-to-bottom without errors** from a fresh clone
**requirements.txt** is present and complete
README.md is **clear and complete** (problem, data, method, findings, setup)
Video is **accessible**, within 5 minutes, and clearly structured
Both URLs submitted via the **course platform** before the deadline
**Course discussion forums** — for technical implementation questions
**Office hours** — for guidance on approach, scope, and methodology
**Documentation** — pandas, scikit-learn, and plotly docs are your best friends
If a dataset is inaccessible, document the issue and use a **Kaggle alternative**
If finishing early, extend your analysis — better visualisations, additional models, new research questions

slides.title: Good Luck!
slides.subtitle: This is your chance to showcase everything you have learned
